API type system enforcement using Zod or similar runtime validation prevents type confusion errors in TypeScript API clients. Problem: TypeScript types are compile-time only; if the API returns a different type than defined, TypeScript is unable to detect it at runtime; null fields that TypeScript says are strings cause runtime errors. Runtime validation with Zod: define schema that matches API response; parse all API responses through schema at runtime; validation throws if response does not match schema; this detects API changes immediately rather than when the incorrect data is used downstream. Benefits: catches API breaking changes immediately on upgrade; provides meaningful error messages when types do not match; generates TypeScript types from schema ensuring compile-time and runtime are consistent; enables safe deserialization with transformation.

API provider changing error response format without notice breaks client error handling code. An API changes from {error: message} to {errors: [{code: X, message: Y}]}; client code that reads error.error now returns undefined; error messages disappear from logs; retry logic that checks specific error codes stops working. Providers should treat error response format as part of the API contract: any change to error format is a breaking change; include error format in OpenAPI spec; version the error format when changing; maintain backward-compatible error format during deprecation period (add new format alongside old format); notify clients via changelog. Defensive client design: write error handling to accept multiple error formats; parse known formats and fall back to toString for unknown; log raw error response body for debugging even when parsing fails.

API integration performance budget establishes acceptable performance thresholds and drives optimization decisions. Budget components: latency budget per endpoint (P50 < 50ms, P95 < 200ms, P99 < 500ms); error rate budget (< 0.1% for all endpoints); availability budget (99.9% uptime = 8.7 hours downtime per year); throughput budget (sustain 1000 requests/second for 10 minutes without degradation). Budget enforcement: automated performance tests run against every release; budget violations fail the build (P99 increased from 400ms to 600ms blocks deployment); capacity planning based on growth projections against current budgets. Budget allocation in distributed system: total user-facing latency budget is allocated across service chain; each service gets a budget slice proportional to its complexity; services that exceed budget must optimize or the chain budget is increased.

API clients hardcoding base URLs fail when the provider migrates domains or adds regional endpoints. A client hardcoded to https://api.example.com fails when the provider moves to https://api.example.io; a client that always hits US endpoint fails for EU users when the provider adds regional endpoints (https://api-eu.example.com) for GDPR compliance. Configuration-driven API base URLs: API base URL in configuration file or environment variable (never in code); staging environment uses sandbox URL, production uses live URL; regional routing controlled by configuration; DNS-based routing handled by the provider; update configuration on domain change without code deployment. Additionally: API client constructor accepts base URL parameter enabling unit tests to point at mock servers; documentation includes base URL configuration instructions prominently.

API integration disaster recovery plan ensures business continuity when third-party APIs experience extended outages. DR plan components per integration: integration criticality classification (P1: payments, P2: email, P3: analytics); maximum acceptable downtime per classification; manual fallback procedures for P1 integrations (manual payment processing offline, batch catch-up when API recovers); provider status page monitoring with escalation alerts; secondary provider assessment for P1 integrations (is there a backup provider we could switch to in 2 hours?); runbook for switching to backup provider; communication templates for user notifications when core functionality is degraded; SLA review schedule to verify provider meets uptime commitments. Test the DR plan quarterly: simulate an outage, execute the fallback procedure, measure time to restore functionality.

Service level agreement monitoring for third-party API dependencies measures whether providers meet their uptime and latency commitments. SLA tracking components: continuous synthetic monitoring making test API calls on 5-minute intervals; availability calculation (successful calls / total calls); latency percentile tracking per interval; incident duration tracking (start when availability drops below SLA threshold, end when recovered); monthly SLA compliance report per provider. SLA comparison: compare measured availability against contracted SLA; document incidents where SLA was violated with duration and business impact; include SLA measurement data in contract renewal negotiations; escalate persistent SLA violations to vendor account team with documented evidence. Proactive monitoring: subscribe to provider status pages; monitor provider-published metrics if available; detect SLA violations before business impact occurs.

Missing API integration documentation for internal teams creates knowledge silos and prevents effective incident response. The engineer who built the integration knows: the authentication mechanism; the retry configuration; which errors are transient vs permanent; the semantic meaning of each response field; known quirks and workarounds. When that engineer is on vacation during an incident, the on-call team is helpless. Integration documentation requirements: purpose and business impact; authentication method and where credentials are stored; base URL and which environments are available; rate limits and current usage level; description of each endpoint used with example request and response; known error codes and their meaning; retry configuration and why; known issues and workarounds; escalation contact at the provider; incident history with resolutions. Store in team documentation system not in the engineer brain.

API integration testing with test doubles at multiple granularities enables fast, reliable, comprehensive test coverage. Test double types for APIs: stubs (return predefined responses; fastest; for unit tests); fakes (simple implementations that work correctly for testing; e.g., in-memory implementation that satisfies the interface); mocks (record calls for verification; assert that specific calls were made); spies (record calls without changing behavior; verify calls without stubbing); contract mocks (generated from API spec; guaranteed to match real API behavior). Granularity selection: unit tests use stubs injected at client level; integration tests use fake local server (WireMock); component tests use contract mocks; end-to-end tests use sandbox environment. Coverage matrix: test that each error condition (rate limit, auth failure, timeout, 5xx) is handled correctly by injecting the condition via stubs in unit tests.

Webhook processing not handling duplicate events causes data inconsistency when the same event is processed multiple times. At-least-once delivery semantics mean the same event may arrive multiple times: provider retries after timeout even if the first attempt succeeded; provider experiences a bug causing duplicate delivery; replay of events for recovery sends previously processed events again. Without deduplication: order.created processed twice creates two orders; payment.succeeded applied twice double-increments account balance; email.send called twice sends the customer two emails. Deduplication implementation: extract unique event ID from webhook payload; check Redis or database for whether this event ID was already processed; if yes, return 200 immediately without processing; if no, process and record the event ID with TTL equal to provider retry window (typically 72 hours); use idempotent operations that are safe to re-execute rather than relying solely on deduplication.

API changelog automation generates structured change logs from OpenAPI specification diffs between versions. Automated changelog generation: compare current OpenAPI spec against previous version; detect added endpoints (new endpoint detected); removed endpoints (deprecated endpoint removed); changed request schemas (field added, removed, made required); changed response schemas (new field, removed field, type change); changed authentication requirements. Classify each change as breaking or non-breaking using predefined rules: removed field = breaking; added optional field = non-breaking; changed type = breaking. Generate human-readable changelog entries from classified changes. Publish to developer portal changelog page automatically on API deployment. Tools: openapi-diff; Zally; custom diffing script using openapi-spec-validator. Automation ensures changelog is never forgotten or incomplete.

API error retry without checking error category causes unnecessary load and delayed failure for non-retryable errors. Retrying a 400 Bad Request with the same malformed payload will never succeed; retrying a 401 Unauthorized without fixing the credentials will never succeed; retrying a 404 Not Found for a resource that was intentionally deleted will never succeed. Each retry wastes compute and delays the code path that would actually handle the error. Error category classification: 4xx client errors are permanent failures (the client must fix the request); retry only for: 429 (wait for rate limit reset), 408 (request timeout, retry may succeed), 409 (conflict, may resolve with retry after delay), 423 (locked, temporary). 5xx server errors are generally retryable: 500 (transient server error), 503 (service unavailable), 504 (gateway timeout). Never retry: 400, 401, 403, 404, 405, 410, 422.

API integration capacity planning estimates required API quota and infrastructure to support projected growth. Quota analysis components: current API calls per day by endpoint; growth rate (month-over-month); seasonal multipliers (peak traffic periods); buffer for traffic spikes (50% above projected average); provider quota limits per API key; number of API keys available. Calculations: projected daily calls 6 months ahead = current_calls * (1 + monthly_growth_rate)^6 * seasonal_multiplier; compare against quota; if projected > 80% of quota, initiate quota increase request or add API keys 3 months before the projected limit is reached. Infrastructure planning: concurrent API calls = requests_per_second * average_latency; connection pool size = concurrent_calls * 1.5; thread pool for async processing = concurrent_calls; monitor actual vs projected to calibrate growth model.

Silent failure from API gateway transformation masking upstream errors confuses debugging. An API gateway configured to return a custom error page for all 5xx responses hides the actual error from clients and developers: the client receives a generic 503 Service Unavailable instead of the specific 500 Internal Server Error from the upstream service; error monitoring shows 503 as the only error type even when the root cause is a 500 from the backend; the correlation ID is lost during transformation; debugging requires examining gateway logs to find the original error. Error passthrough configuration: gateway should preserve upstream status codes rather than transforming all 5xx to 503; include upstream error details in transformed error response; always propagate correlation IDs through transformations; configure monitoring at both gateway and backend levels to preserve visibility.

Testing pyramid for API integrations allocates the most tests to the cheapest level and the fewest to the most expensive. Unit tests (80%): test individual API client methods with mocked HTTP; test error handling with injected error responses; test retry logic; test authentication header injection; test request serialization and response deserialization; run in milliseconds; thousands of tests are practical. Integration tests (15%): test actual API calls to sandbox/staging; test end-to-end flows (create, read, update, delete a resource); test webhook handling with actual delivery; run in seconds to minutes; dozens of tests. Contract tests (4%): verify that mocks match real API behavior; run periodically or on API version updates; one test per major API interaction. End-to-end tests (1%): full user journeys involving multiple API integrations; run in minutes; a handful of tests for critical paths.

API response caching with ETags enables conditional requests that minimize bandwidth while keeping data fresh. ETag workflow: server generates ETag (hash of response content or version identifier) and includes in response header; client stores ETag alongside cached response; on subsequent request, client includes If-None-Match: stored-etag header; server computes current ETag; if identical, returns 304 Not Modified with no body (saves bandwidth); if different, returns 200 with new content and new ETag. Last-Modified alternative: server includes Last-Modified: datetime header; client uses If-Modified-Since: stored-datetime; server returns 304 if unchanged since that time. Implementation: ETag generation must be cheap (avoid full content hash for large responses, use version number or last-modified timestamp); weak ETags (W/) indicate semantic equivalence without byte-for-byte identity.

Cloud provider API integration abstraction assembly for portable cloud operations: (1) StorageAbstraction provides upload, download, list, and delete operations; implemented by S3Adapter (AWS) and GCSAdapter (GCP) with identical interface; (2) QueueAbstraction provides send, receive, and delete message operations; implemented by SQSAdapter and PubSubAdapter; (3) SecretAbstraction provides get and set operations for secrets; implemented by SecretsManagerAdapter and SecretManagerAdapter; (4) ComputeAbstraction provides start, stop, and status for serverless functions; implemented by LambdaAdapter and CloudFunctionsAdapter; (5) DatabaseAbstraction provides CRUD operations; implemented by DynamoDBAdapter and FirestoreAdapter; (6) CredentialProvider handles cloud-native authentication (IAM roles, service accounts) without explicit credentials in code; (7) RetryAdapter normalizes provider-specific transient errors to standard retry signals; (8) MetricsAdapter emits cloud-native metrics normalized to CloudWatch/Cloud Monitoring format.

Returning HTTP 200 with an "error" field in the body instead of using appropriate 4xx/5xx status codes breaks all standard HTTP tooling, monitoring, and client error handling. Load balancers, API gateways, and monitoring systems classify responses by HTTP status; a 200 with embedded error appears as a success to all infrastructure, silently masking failures. Circuit breakers never trip, alerting never fires, and clients must parse every body to detect errors instead of checking status codes. This anti-pattern originates from legacy SOAP-over-HTTP ports where the transport was always 200. REST APIs must use 4xx for client errors (400 Bad Request, 401 Unauthorized, 403 Forbidden, 404 Not Found, 409 Conflict, 422 Unprocessable Entity) and 5xx for server errors, with a consistent error body schema (RFC 7807 Problem Details recommended). Audit all API responses and reject any endpoint that returns 2xx for error conditions.

API versioning via URL path prefix (/v1/, /v2/) provides the clearest visibility and simplest client implementation but couples version to resource URL identity. Header-based versioning (Accept: application/vnd.api+json;version=2 or custom X-API-Version header) keeps URLs stable but requires clients to set headers explicitly and makes versions invisible in browser navigation and log analysis. Content negotiation versioning (using Accept header media type parameters) is most RESTfully correct but most complex. Organizations with developer portals and public APIs typically choose URL versioning for discoverability. Internal microservices often prefer header versioning to avoid URL proliferation. The critical rule is: commit to one strategy from day one, document it in OpenAPI specs, and ensure all clients pin to explicit versions rather than defaulting to latest.

Webhook event delivery guarantees must be explicitly documented and matched by consumer idempotency logic. At-most-once delivery (fire and forget) is simplest but risks missed events during consumer downtime. At-least-once delivery (retry until acknowledged) is standard for critical events but requires consumers to handle duplicates. Exactly-once is effectively impossible at HTTP layer; it requires idempotency keys plus deduplication windows on the consumer side. Most webhook providers (Stripe, GitHub, SendGrid) implement at-least-once with HMAC-SHA256 signatures and expect consumers to deduplicate by event ID. Consumers must store processed event IDs for at least the provider's retry window (typically 72 hours). Failure to implement deduplication causes double-charges, duplicate notifications, and phantom state transitions in production workflows.

The Backends for Frontends (BFF) pattern creates a dedicated API layer for each client type (web, iOS, Android, third-party) rather than exposing a single general-purpose API to all. Each BFF is owned by the frontend team, aggregates multiple microservice calls into single client-optimized responses, applies client-specific data transformations, and manages authentication flows appropriate to the client. BFF eliminates over-fetching and under-fetching by returning exactly the fields each client needs, reduces round trips by colocating aggregation logic close to microservices, and allows each client type to evolve independently. The BFF layer is thin — it should orchestrate and transform, not contain business logic. Shared business logic belongs in downstream microservices. BFF is particularly valuable when mobile clients have different bandwidth constraints and UX requirements than web clients.

Not validating TLS certificate chains in API client code creates man-in-the-middle attack vectors in production. Common anti-patterns include: `verify=False` in Python requests, `rejectUnauthorized: false` in Node.js https options, `-k` flags left in curl commands baked into scripts, and SDK initialization with certificate validation disabled for "testing" that ships to production. These settings are often added to bypass self-signed certificate errors in development and accidentally committed. Attackers on the same network segment can intercept all API traffic including credentials and sensitive data. All production API clients must enforce certificate validation with the system CA bundle. For internal services using private CAs, distribute the private CA certificate to all clients via configuration management — never disable validation. Automated security scanning must flag `verify=False` patterns in CI.

A production-grade async job API combines four components: submission endpoint, status polling endpoint, result retrieval endpoint, and optional webhook notification. POST /jobs returns 202 Accepted with a Location header pointing to GET /jobs/{id}. The status endpoint returns machine-readable status (pending/running/completed/failed) plus progress percentage and estimated completion. On completion, GET /jobs/{id}/result returns the output with appropriate caching headers. Webhook notification triggers when status changes to terminal state, allowing clients to avoid polling. Rate limit status endpoint calls aggressively (clients should use exponential backoff with 1s/2s/4s/8s intervals). Set job TTL and document it — results older than 24 hours return 410 Gone. This pattern handles long-running operations (report generation, data exports, ML inference) without holding HTTP connections open.

Migrating a REST API from v1 to v2 without breaking existing clients requires: (1) Deploy v2 endpoint alongside v1 — never replace, always add. (2) Update API gateway routing so /v2/ routes to new implementation while /v1/ routes to existing code. (3) Publish migration guide with diff of breaking changes and code examples for every changed endpoint. (4) Instrument both versions with request count metrics and set a sunset timeline (minimum 6 months for external APIs, minimum 3 months for internal). (5) Send deprecation notice via registered email and return Deprecation and Sunset headers on v1 responses (RFC 8594). (6) Monitor v1 traffic decay; if usage plateaus, reach out to active consumers individually. (7) When v1 traffic reaches <1% of peak, announce final sunset date with 30-day notice. (8) After sunset, return 410 Gone instead of removing routes immediately.

Server-Sent Events (SSE) provides a simple unidirectional streaming channel from server to client over standard HTTP, making it ideal for real-time feed updates, live dashboards, and progress notifications where the client only needs to receive data. SSE uses chunked transfer encoding with text/event-stream content type and supports automatic reconnection with Last-Event-ID header for resuming after disconnect. Unlike WebSockets, SSE works through HTTP/1.1 proxies and load balancers without special configuration, uses standard HTTP authentication, and requires no protocol upgrade. SSE is preferable to WebSockets when communication is one-directional (server pushes only), deployments involve HTTP/1.1 proxies, or operational simplicity matters. WebSockets are necessary only for bidirectional real-time communication (chat, collaborative editing). SSE connections consume server file descriptors; implement connection limits and heartbeat timeouts.

Exposing internal database IDs as API resource identifiers leaks implementation details and creates security vulnerabilities. Sequential integer IDs allow enumeration attacks — an attacker can iterate through all user IDs to harvest data or test access controls. Internal IDs expose the scale and growth rate of your system (user ID 847 reveals early customer count). Database sharding migrations require changing IDs which breaks all existing API consumers and bookmarks. The correct pattern is to use stable public identifiers — either UUIDs (v4 for random, v7 for time-ordered), or opaque slugs — as the external API identifier, with a mapping layer to internal database IDs. Alternatively, enforce strict authorization checks so enumeration of valid IDs provides no additional access. All new APIs must use UUID or equivalent non-sequential identifiers; migrating existing APIs requires a deprecation cycle.

API design should be driven by consumer use cases, not by internal data model structure. A common mistake is creating CRUD endpoints that mirror database tables (GET /orders/{id}/order_line_items/{item_id}/tax_calculations/{calc_id}) which forces consumers to make many sequential requests to assemble meaningful data. Consumer-driven API design starts by documenting consumer workflows: what information does the consumer need to complete each task? Then design endpoints that return that information in one response. This often means creating composite/aggregate endpoints that join multiple internal entities, returning denormalized data optimized for read performance. The internal microservice boundary and data model can be completely different from the API surface. GraphQL formalizes this approach by letting consumers specify exactly what fields they need. REST can achieve the same with thoughtfully designed resource representations and sparse fieldsets (JSON:API spec).
